metadata.file_role: Unusual, wood-inhabiting, parasitic, gilled, and veined bolete forms
metadata.purpose: Boletinellus, Buchwaldoboletus, Paragyrodon, Phylloporus, Pseudoboletus, Pseudomerulius, and related unusual Boletales forms.
metadata.organization_rule: Physical file placement follows stable biological or morphological identity. Useful parts, routes, safety, and seasonal behavior remain record-level fields.

records.culinary_uses: Edible but not usually ranked with choice boletes; use only young, firm specimens after confident ID near ash.
records.edibility_detail: A recognizable ash-associated bolete. Quality is modest; older specimens soften quickly.

mushroom_profile.summary: Yellow-brown to reddish-brown bolete growing from soil near ash; cap often wavy or irregular, stem often off-center, and the large shallow angular pores are radially arranged and bruise olive-brown to bluish.
staining: Pores bruise brownish to olive; flesh may show blue-green tones

records.field_identification: Yellow-brown to reddish-brown bolete growing from soil near ash; cap often wavy or irregular, stem often off-center, and the large shallow angular pores are radially arranged and bruise olive-brown to bluish.
records.look_alike_notes: The ash association and wide, radially stretched pores separate it from many ordinary boletes; old man of the woods is darker and shaggier, while parasitic bolete grows on earthballs.
records.edibility_notes: Edible but not usually ranked with choice boletes; use only young, firm specimens after confident ID near ash.

edible_use.notes: Edible but not usually ranked with choice boletes; use only young, firm specimens after confident ID near ash.

lookalike_risk.label: High — bolete confusion, severe-GI/bitter look-alikes possible
lookalike_risk.short_label: High — bolete confusion, severe-GI/bitter look-alikes possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Edible bolete use needs pore color, staining, host/substrate, taste where appropriate, and separation from false king, bitter Tylopilus, and red-pored/fast-blue-staining caution boletes.
required_checks: Reject bitter taste where safe nibble-and-spit is appropriate
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Do not eat unidentified boletes; red/orange pores, bitter taste, or violent blue staining mean stop.

dangerous_lookalikes.warning: False king bolete is a northern/Great Lakes porcini-type danger concern.
target_traits: Pale netting near upper stem when expected
lookalike_traits: Hemlock or northern Great Lakes false-king concern
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: If false king traits are possible, do not eat.

target_traits: White/yellow pores without bitter Tylopilus posture
required_checks: Tiny nibble-and-spit only if you already know the mushroom is safe to taste
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Bitter taste means not food; one specimen can ruin the pan.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Poisonous / not recommended red-pored blue-staining bolete group.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Red/orange pores or fast strong blue staining mean stop unless expert-confirmed.

records.source_note: v4.3.210 adds the source-backed macro spore-print description and normalized color filters; spore color alone is not proof of species identity or edibility.

records.culinary_uses: Not recommended as a foraging food in this guide; included for recognition of an odd wood-associated bolete group.

mushroom_profile.summary: Distinct North American Buchwaldoboletus documented from Michigan, typically clustered on pine sawdust, pine stumps, or similar lignicolous settings; exact macro print and UP occurrence remain unresolved.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer to fall where recorded.

boleteSubgroup: Odd ecology / wood or unusual hymenium
records.edibility_detail: Use for ID/caution only until the exact species concept and local relevance are verified.
records.mushroom_card_note: Yellow wood-associated Buchwaldoboletus with bluing flesh; use as a taxonomy-aware comparison record, not a food target.
records.field_identification: Yellow to yellow-brown bolete on or near pine wood/sawdust or wood-decay situations, with yellow pores/stem, yellow basal mycelium, and flesh that can bruise blue. Compare carefully with Buchwaldoboletus hemichrysus and B. lignicola; taxonomy and names in this group are tangled.
records.taxonomy_note: USDA Forest Service survey supports B. sphaerocephalus as distinct from B. hemichrysus and B. lignicola in North America, including Michigan.

use_links.label: Wikimedia Commons: Buchwaldoboletus sphaerocephalus
use_links.notes: Media/taxon context only; not used as final proof for taxonomy, ecology, range, or spore-print claims.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Buchwaldoboletus hemichrysus comparison
use_links.notes: Different species retained only for comparison; not proof for B. sphaerocephalus.

use_links.label: USDA Forest Service: Buchwaldoboletus survey
use_links.notes: Exact North American species concept, Michigan occurrence, pine-sawdust/stump ecology, and separation from related wood boletes.

records.culinary_uses: Occasional edible in some guides; not a prime target.

mushroom_profile.summary: Gilled bolete with bright yellow gills and white basal mycelium; compare carefully with P. rhodoxanthus, which has yellow basal mycelium.
research_notes: Looks like a bolete from above but has gills. Fresh gills are bright yellow, and the basal mycelium should be white rather than yellow. Compare with P. rhodoxanthus; Hypomyces can obscure the stem base.

boleteSubgroup: Odd ecology / wood or unusual hymenium
records.edibility_detail: Use only with confident gilled-bolete ID. Fresh yellow gills and white basal mycelium are key; separate carefully from P. rhodoxanthus and other gilled-bolete concepts.
records.mushroom_card_note: Gilled bolete with bright yellow gills and white basal mycelium; cap 2–6 cm across; compare carefully with P. rhodoxanthus, which has yellow basal mycelium.
records.field_identification: Looks like a bolete from above but has gills. Fresh gills are bright yellow, and the basal mycelium should be white rather than yellow. Compare with P. rhodoxanthus; Hypomyces can obscure the stem base. Size: cap 2–6 cm across; stem 20–50 mm long and 2–10 mm thick.
records.edibility_notes: Use only with confident gilled-bolete ID. Fresh yellow gills and white basal mycelium are key; separate carefully from P. rhodoxanthus and other gilled-bolete concepts.

images_structured.author: walt sturgeon (Mycowalt) / Mushroom Observer
images_structured.credit: walt sturgeon (Mycowalt) / Mushroom Observer, CC BY-SA 3.0, via Wikimedia Commons
images_structured.rights_notes: Exact author/license metadata verified from the Wikimedia Commons source page; unverified extra images were dropped from visible image arrays in v4.3.157.

records.taxonomy_notes: Generic “Gilled Bolete” should not be used as a clickable target because multiple Phylloporus records share that common label. This record is intentionally dual-listed under Gilled and Spongelike/Boletes because Phylloporus are gilled boletes.
records.image_rights_audit_note: Dropped unverified 59237 and 103301 images; exact source-page author/license verification was not available in the worker pass.

field_measurements.stem: 20–50 mm long; 2–10 mm thick / about 0.8–2 in long; about 0.08–0.4 in thick
field_measurements.notes: Size is source-backed from MushroomExpert; separate from P. rhodoxanthus by white basal mycelium, noting Hypomyces can obscure the base.

records.source_note: v4.3.210 removes the unsupported white spore-print claim. A direct exact-species macro-print source is still needed, and microscopy remains important for separation.

records.culinary_uses: Occasional edible in some guides; not a prime target.

mushroom_profile.summary: Common eastern gilled bolete with bright yellow gills and yellow basal mycelium; cap can be red to brown.
staining: Usually little to no bluing, occasional bluing reported
research_notes: Bolete-like cap and stem but with bright yellow gills. Yellow basal mycelium helps separate it from P. leucomycelinus, though Hypomyces can hide the base. Associated with hardwoods east of the Rockies.

boleteSubgroup: Odd ecology / wood or unusual hymenium
records.edibility_detail: Keep the ID conservative because gilled-bolete concepts are shifting. Confirm the bolete-like cap, bright yellow gills, yellow basal mycelium, hardwood association, and lack of dangerous look-alike traits.
records.mushroom_card_note: Gilled bolete with bright yellow gills and yellow basal mycelium; cap can be red to brown and usually 2–8.5 cm across.
records.field_identification: Bolete-like cap and stem but with bright yellow gills. Yellow basal mycelium helps separate it from P. leucomycelinus, though Hypomyces can hide the base. Associated with hardwoods east of the Rockies. Size: cap usually 2–8.5 cm across, occasionally larger; stem 3–8 cm long and up to 1 cm thick.
records.edibility_notes: Keep the ID conservative because gilled-bolete concepts are shifting. Confirm the bolete-like cap, bright yellow gills, yellow basal mycelium, hardwood association, and lack of dangerous look-alike traits.

records.taxonomy_notes: Generic “Gilled Bolete” should not be used as a clickable target because multiple Phylloporus records share that common label. This record is intentionally dual-listed under Gilled and Spongelike/Boletes because Phylloporus are gilled boletes.
records.image_rights_audit_note: Dropped unverified 342248 and Gilled Bolete images; exact source-page author/license verification was not available in the worker pass.

field_measurements.cap: 2–8.5 cm across, occasionally to 13 cm / about 0.8–3.3 in, occasionally to 5.1 in
field_measurements.stem: 3–8 cm long; up to 1 cm thick / about 1.2–3.1 in long; up to 0.4 in thick
field_measurements.notes: MushroomExpert describes this as variable in size; small fruiting bodies are possible, but cap size can run larger than the list thumbnail may suggest.

records.source_note: v4.3.210 adds the source-backed macro spore-print description and normalized color filters; spore color alone is not proof of species identity or edibility.

records.culinary_uses: Not treated as a food target; useful as a Great Lakes specialty ID species.

mushroom_profile.summary: Great Lakes-region oak bolete with sticky brownish cap, yellow pores that brown strongly, and nearly round spores.

boleteSubgroup: Odd ecology / wood or unusual hymenium
records.mushroom_card_note: Great Lakes-region oak bolete with sticky brownish cap, yellow pores that brown strongly, and nearly round spores.
records.field_identification: Look for a sticky yellow-brown to brown cap, yellow pore surface that bruises dark brown, a sheathing veil/ring zone low on the stem, oak association, and nearly round spores. Known especially from Great Lakes oak settings.
records.edibility_detail: ID/comparison entry, not a recommended edible. Confirm sticky brownish cap, yellow pores that bruise dark brown, oak association, and the unusual veil/ring-zone clues.

use_links.notes: Great Lakes/oak habitat, Michigan note, field traits, and spore traits.

records.edibility_notes: ID/comparison entry, not a recommended edible. Confirm sticky brownish cap, yellow pores that bruise dark brown, oak association, and the unusual veil/ring-zone clues.

records.culinary_uses: Not recommended as food in this guide; valuable mainly as a curiosity/caution species.

mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Parasitic Bolete.
staining: Little to no staining; may stain dirty brown slowly
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall, when earthballs are present.
research_notes: Key field clue remains growth directly on Scleroderma earthballs.

records.general_notes: Useful as a caution/curiosity species because the growth habit is distinctive: a bolete growing on another fungus.
records.edibility_detail: Avoid for food here because it grows directly on Scleroderma earthballs, which are poisonous. Confirm the parasitic habit on an earthball before naming it.
records.field_identification: Small yellow-brown bolete that grows directly from common earthballs (Scleroderma), usually the most important ID feature. Pores are yellow to olive and the stem is small and often curved.

use_links.label: Wikimedia Commons: Pseudoboletus parasiticus
use_links.notes: Large public media set and taxon category.

records.edibility_notes: Avoid for food here because it grows directly on Scleroderma earthballs, which are poisonous. Confirm the parasitic habit on an earthball before naming it.

records.culinary_uses: Not recommended as food; useful as a wood-growing Boletales oddball.

mushroom_profile.summary: Wood-growing Boletales oddball with a veined/merulioid yellow-orange fertile surface; not a normal cap-and-stem bolete target.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer to fall where observed.
research_notes: v4.3.160 cleared unresolved Commons image paths; verified exact-species image recovery remains open.

boleteSubgroup: Odd ecology / wood or unusual hymenium
records.mushroom_card_note: Wood-growing Boletales oddball with a veined/merulioid yellow-orange fertile surface; not a normal cap-and-stem bolete target.
records.field_identification: Look for a sessile or reduced-stem fruit body on dead/debarked conifer wood, with a fuzzy yellow cap surface and wavy, gill-like to veined yellow-orange underside rather than round bolete pores. Keep in the Other lane, not the main bolete lane.
records.edibility_detail: Keep as an identification/form record, not a forage target. Confirm the wood-associated fruitbody and strongly veined to merulioid underside.

use_links.label: FungiKingdom: Pseudomerulius curtisii
use_links.notes: Wood substrate and veined yellow-orange fertile surface.

use_links.label: Wikimedia Commons: Pseudomerulius
use_links.notes: Genus category with Pseudomerulius curtisii media.

records.edibility_notes: Keep as an identification/form record, not a forage target. Confirm the wood-associated fruitbody and strongly veined to merulioid underside.
records.image_rights_audit_note: v4.3.160 cleared raw unresolved Commons image paths; replacement candidates were not used without exact-species verification.

records.culinary_uses: Not recommended as food; useful as an odd wood-associated bolete record.

mushroom_profile.summary: Distinct wood-associated Buchwaldoboletus on dead conifer wood, often linked with Phaeolus schweinitzii; exact macro print and Michigan/UP occurrence remain unresolved.

boleteSubgroup: Odd ecology / wood or unusual hymenium
records.mushroom_card_note: Wood-associated Buchwaldoboletus tied to conifer wood and possibly Phaeolus schweinitzii; keep as an oddball ID record.
records.field_identification: Yellow-brown cap, yellow pore surface, wood association, and possible relationship with the dyer’s polypore Phaeolus schweinitzii are the useful clues. Compare with B. hemichrysus and B. sphaerocephalus.
records.edibility_detail: ID/comparison only in this guide. Confirm wood association and compare carefully with B. hemichrysus and B. sphaerocephalus.

use_links.notes: Exact North American taxonomy, conifer-wood ecology, and distinction from B. hemichrysus and B. sphaerocephalus.

records.edibility_notes: ID/comparison only in this guide. Confirm wood association and compare carefully with B. hemichrysus and B. sphaerocephalus.
records.taxonomy_note: USDA Forest Service survey supports B. lignicola as distinct from B. hemichrysus and B. sphaerocephalus.
